issues_found: Source body was truncated, ending mid-sentence at 'pass-through rate for no' — the August 2025 non-importer pass-through conclusion is missing from the provided text. The record correctly captures importer pass-through but this gap limits full verification.

Publication age (2025-09-29) is approximately 6 months before TODAY (2026-04-01), exceeding the 90-day threshold. The record appropriately notes this but the source still has archival value given its empirical findings on tariff pass-through mechanisms.
human_review_reason: The record is well-constructed and accurately summarizes the source's key findings. Minor uncertainty stems from source truncation (missing non-importer August conclusion) and publication age, but these do not materially undermine the record's accuracy. Auto-routing to review_queue is appropriate.

corrected_fields.summary: A Boston Fed survey of 500-600 small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) conducted December 2024 to August 2025 finds that SMBs significantly raised their tariff expectations from roughly 19% to 25% on average during the first four months of 2025. Actual tariff rates paid by affected importers nearly doubled from 6.5% in January 2025 to 11.4% in July 2025. The authors estimate a 0.75% near-term increase in core consumer prices from tariff costs on directly imported goods, with importers indicating they would pass through about two-thirds of anticipated cost increases into prices within one year. SMBs expecting tariffs to persist over a year planned to pass through up to three times more cost increases than those expecting short-lived tariffs.
corrected_fields.why_it_matters: This research provides direct survey evidence on how businesses intend to transmit tariff costs to consumer prices, offering a forward-looking channel for tariff pass-through into inflation. The finding that over 45% of affected SMBs expect tariff impacts to persist beyond one year suggests businesses are pricing in sustained trade policy changes rather than temporary disruptions. This has implications for the path of core inflation and Federal Reserve policy considerations, though the authors explicitly note these are their own views and not official Federal Reserve positions.
corrected_fields.macro_context: The survey provides empirical evidence for the inflation transmission mechanism of tariffs. The estimated 0.75% near-term increase in core CPI from direct tariff pass-through represents a meaningful but contained inflation impulse from this specific channel. The distinction between short-duration vs. persistent tariff expectations driving a 3x difference in planned pass-through rates suggests that perceived tariff duration is a critical determinant of business pricing behavior. The passthrough rate jump from ~1/3 baseline to ~2/3 for importers following the April 2025 reciprocal tariff announcement indicates businesses adjust pricing strategies as policy uncertainty resolves.
